epics.next_action: Legacy epic — shipped pre-spec-template convention. See spec.md stub + stories.json.
Closed reopen-remediation in f53eacf (AC-039 fix + AC-047 spec amendment). AC-045 scenario 5 / B3 retry-attempts flake tracked as separate finding.
Architectural refactor — run /base:arch-debate first; do not /base:feature directly until the receive-engine boundary is debated.
Phase A+B complete 2026-07-07; v2 (dark themes, more elements) remains out of scope, not yet planned

findings.text: base:e2e-testing skill runs in a forked context but returns only 'Skill execution completed' to the parent — no pass/fail counts, no failure messages. Caller has to re-run the runner directly to learn whether tests passed.

DM e2e specs (dm-giftwrap-bell, dm-historical-recovery, dm-third-party-inbound) publish gift-wraps / kind-4 events directly to strfry via raw WebSocket from the test process. Violates the publish-via-app guideline (see CLAUDE.md "E2E tests"): tests pass even when the app's NDK/publish path is broken. Rewrite to publish through a second browser.newContext() that boots the peer and calls the app's publish helper. Open question for dm-third-party-inbound: the app likely has no kind-4 NIP-04 publish path; either confirm an existing UI/bridge route, or document this spec as the narrow exception with a fixture-loader pattern rather than inline WebSocket.

The __nostlingPublishDm test bridge lives in unreadStore.ts but has nothing to do with unread counts (module cohesion smell, flagged in DM walled-garden Opus review). Relocating it out of scope for this epic (risk of regressions). Revisit in a dedicated cleanup pass.
Extract DM profileMap builder; tidy feedback-mode prop coordination in ContactChat
findings.motivation: The feedback channel epic surfaced a pre-existing duplication and some prop-coordination spread. The two-entry DM profileMap (self + one peer) is built near-identically in app/pages/feedback.tsx and app/pages/contacts.tsx ContactDetailView, with the 'own user' entry byte-for-byte identical. ContactChat's feedback-mode behaviour is split across two files (feedback.tsx passes source + composerPlaceholder; ContactChat derives image/reaction gating from source), so the coordinated props can silently drift. None of this is a bug today, but the duplication predates the epic and the feedback page added a third near-copy of the profileMap, so the cost will keep growing as more DM surfaces appear.
findings.behavior: app/pages/feedback.tsx (profileMap ~lines 43-56) and app/pages/contacts.tsx ContactDetailView (profileMap ~lines 220-233) independently construct {[selfHex]:{...}, [peerHex]:{...}} MemberProfile maps; the self entry (nickname: ownProfile.nickname || truncateNpub(pubkeyToNpub(self)), avatar: ownProfile.avatar, updatedAt: new Date().toISOString()) is duplicated verbatim. Separately, ContactChat repeats the 'source === "feedback"' check three times (send markers, onReact gating, allowImageAttachments).
findings.approach: 1) Extract buildDmProfileMap({ selfHex, selfProfile, peerHex, peerNickname, peerAvatar, peerUpdatedAt? }) into a shared module (e.g. app/src/lib/contacts.ts or a profile helper) and call it from both feedback.tsx and contacts.tsx ContactDetailView — this is the high-value, low-risk extraction touching a pre-existing duplication. 2) Optionally hoist 'const isFeedback = source === "feedback"' in ContactChat for readability — but KEEP the three send-path gates (markers, onReact, allowImageAttachments + the paste/drop guards) as separate explicit checks: they guard independent send paths and their separateness is what makes each path auditable (do NOT merge them). 3) Do NOT introduce a <FeedbackChat> wrapper yet — wait for a second feedback-like surface; one call site does not justify the indirection.
findings.success_signal: feedback.tsx and contacts.tsx ContactDetailView both call one buildDmProfileMap helper; no duplicated self-profile literal; the three feedback send-path gates remain separate and explicit.

findings.text: marmot-ts 0.5.1 drops the MLS sender-leaf credential for application messages — inner rumor pubkey is not identity-bound to the authenticated sender
findings.motivation: AC-AUTH-2 group clause and the Marmot spec (group-messaging.md:43 'receivers validate the inner app event pubkey matches the MLS-authenticated identity') are UNMET: ts-mls returns only {message:Uint8Array} for applicationMessage (sender leaf dropped; proposals keep it), and unprotectPrivateMessage is not exported. Any current group member can forge the inner pubkey to impersonate another member — forged delete/edit (this epic) and forged kind-9/kind-7 (pre-existing). MLS still bars non-members.
findings.behavior: Group delete/edit authorizes on the self-asserted inner rumor pubkey (S3 pubkeysMatch vs target author); marmot-ts never verifies that pubkey against the sending MLS leaf, so a malicious member can forge another member's signal.
findings.approach: Upstream: get marmot-ts/ts-mls to surface the authenticated sender leaf credential pubkey for application messages, then verify rumor.pubkey === senderCredentialPubkey in applicationRumorDispatcher for ALL kinds (also closes the pre-existing kind-9/kind-7 impersonation hole). Product decision 2026-07-07: ship group edit/delete with documented group-member-attested trust; this finding tracks the upstream fix.
findings.success_signal: applicationRumorDispatcher rejects an application rumor whose inner pubkey != the MLS-authenticated sender leaf credential, proven by a forged-pubkey test

findings.text: Share contact card permanently fails when the active NIP-46 signer's pubkey differs from the local identity
findings.motivation: NostrIdentityContext.tsx:295 accepts a NIP-46 bunker whose reported pubkey differs from the local identity, logging only a console.warn (no hard block, by design for this epic slice). But encodeCard's signer-pubkey guard (contactCard.ts:272-273) throws whenever signed.pubkey !== pubkeyHex, and handleOpenShareCard (settings.tsx:374-402) catches that throw and always shows the generic copy.identity.shareCardError. In that identity-mismatch state, 'Share contact card' can never succeed no matter how many times the user retries, and the error copy gives no indication why or what to do.
findings.behavior: handleOpenShareCard calls getOwnShareCard with pubkeyHex: identityPubkeyHex (the local identity's pubkey) but getSignEvent may resolve to a NIP-46 signer whose actual signing key differs. encodeCard's guard (correctly, for privacy/integrity) refuses to sign a card claiming a pubkey the active signer cannot back, and throws. The UI has no distinct code path for this case -- it lands in the same catch as any other share failure and shows a generic retry-implying message.
findings.approach: Either (a) surface a distinct, actionable error when the guard-mismatch throw is caught (e.g. a message naming the signer/identity mismatch instead of a generic retry message), or (b) tighten NostrIdentityContext's accept-with-warning behavior at connect time so a mismatched bunker is rejected before it becomes the active signer, closing the gap at the source instead of downstream at share time.
findings.success_signal: Mismatched-bunker state either cannot become active, or Share failure copy names the mismatch instead of a generic error.

findings.text: decodeCard accepts a signed name_len=0 card that encodeCard never emits, opening an LWW empty-name clobber path
findings.motivation: encodeCard only sets the SIGNED flag (and only emits created_at/name_len/sig) when the user has a nickname (DD 10: 'No nickname -> share an unsigned card'), so the encoder path never itself produces a signed card with name_len=0. But decodeCard's structural check at line 343 only rejects nameLen > MAX_NAME_BYTES -- it accepts nameLen === 0 on a signed card as long as the signature verifies. A hand-crafted (or future non-Few client's) self-signed empty-name card with a newer created_at than an existing cache entry would pass verification and, per AC-CACHE-2's LWW rule, overwrite an existing cached nickname with the empty string via importCard -- a legitimately-signed card silently blanking a name the recipient had already cached from a richer source (e.g. MLS profile sync).
findings.behavior: decodeCard treats a signed, correctly-verifying, empty-name (name_len=0) card as a fully valid v1 signed card and returns profile: { nickname: '', updatedAt }. Nothing in the encode/decode/parse/cache pipeline currently distinguishes 'signed empty name, meant to clear the cached name' from 'signed empty name, an encoder/decoder-disagreement edge case nobody meant to produce'.
findings.approach: Pick one of two explicit resolutions: (1) decodeCard rejects name_len=0 on a signed card as malformed, restoring encoder/decoder symmetry; or (2) keep it valid but document it as an intentional 'clear my cached name' affordance and add an AC + test asserting the LWW-clobber-to-empty behavior is desired, not accidental.
findings.success_signal: encodeCard and decodeCard agree on whether a signed empty-name card is valid, backed by a test for the chosen behavior.

findings.text: AC-INTL-1 i18n parity for contact-card-exchange relies on a hand-maintained key list that won't catch future en-only additions
findings.motivation: addPage.i18n.test.ts satisfies AC-INTL-1 for this epic (S3-S7) via a REQUIRED_KEYS array reconciled by hand against 'git diff HEAD -- app/src/lib/i18n.ts' for this epic's working tree -- the same REQUIRED_KEYS discipline other *.i18n.test.ts suites use. A repo-wide programmatic parity walk was deliberately rejected during S7 (opus-reviewer + verification-examiner) because it would fail on pre-existing, out-of-epic en/de divergence elsewhere in i18n.ts. The tradeoff was accepted for this epic but the durability gap is real: a future epic that adds an en-only key and forgets to extend some REQUIRED_KEYS list gets no automated signal.
findings.behavior: i18n parity across the whole app is enforced only by however many per-epic *.i18n.test.ts suites happen to exist, each with its own manually maintained REQUIRED_KEYS array; there is no single mechanism that would catch a newly-added en-only string key anywhere in app/src/lib/i18n.ts.
findings.approach: Design a repo-wide i18n parity test that asserts en/de key-set equality for app/src/lib/i18n.ts's Copy type as a whole, while allowlisting (or fixing) the pre-existing divergence that currently blocks that approach, so future epics get automatic coverage instead of needing their own hand-maintained REQUIRED_KEYS list.
findings.success_signal: A single i18n parity test covers the whole Copy type (or an explicit allowlist of pre-existing gaps), and a new en-only key fails it automatically.

findings.text: Groups e2e suite is flaky under shared-relay/shared-keypair cross-spec contamination
findings.motivation: The full e2e gate (make test-e2e-all) is the project's only definitive gate, but ~7 groups tests intermittently fail on cross-user relay-propagation assertions. Observed 2026-07-10: 7 failures in a full run AND 6 failures re-running just 5 of those spec files on a freshly-wiped relay — i.e. reproduces at low load, independent of any single feature change. This makes the gate unreliable and forces ship-over-red judgment calls.
findings.behavior: Specs reuse deterministic USER_A/USER_B keypairs against one shared strfry relay for the whole run; gift-wraps/pending-invitations from earlier specs contaminate later specs' queues, so propagation assertions (member profile name, poll announcement, image lightbox, nickname update, feedback reply) time out nondeterministically.
findings.approach: Options: per-spec unique ephemeral keypairs; or per-spec-file relay wipe; or baseline-then-delta assertions that tolerate stale queue entries (drain-by-eventId rather than .last()). Pick one and apply suite-wide.
findings.success_signal: make test-e2e-all is green across 3 consecutive runs with no per-spec quarantine

findings.text: Make the icon set a real, exercised theme dimension. It is modeled as a per-theme treatment (manifest.treatments.iconSet: line|filled|pixel) but only 'line' is ever rendered — aquarelle is the sole registered theme — so the 'filled' (Phosphor fill) and 'pixel' (pixelarticons) columns of ICON_SETS are dead data that silently bit-rots.
findings.motivation: Icon family is modeled as a per-theme treatment (ThemeIcon resolves manifest.treatments.iconSet against ICON_SETS), implying themes were meant to vary it. In practice there is one registered theme (aquarelle=line) after commit 8fae18e purged the rest, so 'filled' and 'pixel' are never rendered. Dead branches drift: swapping the live 'line' set from Phosphor to Lucide (2026-07-11) left filled=ph:*-fill and pixel=pixelarticons:* untouched, so a future theme selecting 'filled' or 'pixel' would get a family inconsistent with 'line' and with each other, and no test guards this.
findings.behavior: resolveIconId only ever runs with iconSet='line'; the filled/pixel columns of every ICON_SETS entry are unreachable. No registered theme selects them and no coverage asserts the three columns stay mutually consistent.
findings.approach: Decide product intent: (a) icon family becomes a themed/selectable dimension — add at least one theme that uses filled or pixel (or expose it in settings) so all three columns are exercised, plus a test asserting each ICON_SETS entry defines all three coherently; or (b) drop the multi-set abstraction — collapse ICON_SETS to the single active family and remove IconSetName, simplifying ThemeIcon. (a) is the 'make it part of a theme' direction requested here.
findings.success_signal: Either every IconSetName column is rendered by some registered theme (guarded by a test), or the unused columns are removed and ThemeIcon no longer branches on a set that never varies.

findings.text: Gate incoming calls with the block deny-list (a blocked contact can still ring)
findings.motivation: A blocked contact can still ring the user via an incoming call: the call gate uses the bare isAllowedDmSender and block deliberately keeps the peer in knownPeers (spec §9 / ADR-005). 'I blocked them but they can still call me' is a user-facing surprise. Surfaced by the block-contact epic review (2026-07-14); user chose to track as a follow-up. Latent while calls are disabled.
findings.behavior: IncomingCallWatcher.tsx:149 gates on isAllowedDmSender only; a blocked-but-known peer passes and can ring.
findings.approach: Compose isBlockedPeer into the call gate (mirror the DM isAllowedDmSenderComposite), with call-specific ACs + e2e in a dedicated story. Re-enable-calls dependent.

findings.text: Block-revision (and sibling revision counters) have no cross-tab sync
findings.motivation: If a user blocks a peer in one tab while another tab has that peer's ContactChat mounted, the second tab's blockedPeersRef never refreshes and keeps persisting the blocked peer's messages — a privacy gap. Pre-existing app-wide characteristic (knownPeersRevision, groupDataVersion share it); surfaced by the block-contact epic review (2026-07-14).
findings.behavior: blockedPeersRevision is an in-memory useState counter with no storage-event / BroadcastChannel cross-tab propagation; a block in another tab is not observed until reload.
findings.approach: Add a BroadcastChannel or window 'storage' event sync for the revision counters so block/unblock (and known-peer changes) propagate across tabs. Project-wide hardening, not block-specific.

findings.text: One-time migration: clear stale unread badges for legacy-hidden (now blocked) contacts
findings.motivation: Contacts hidden before the block-contact epic become 'blocked' but their pre-existing unread counts linger on the bell with no way to clear them (their chat is not openable to mark read). Product decision (2026-07-14) was to KEEP their DM history (no retroactive wipe), but the stale unread badge should still be cleared. Minor cosmetic wart affecting only the legacy-hidden-with-unreads cohort.
findings.behavior: initDirectMessageCounts prunes with raw isAllowedDmSender, which blocked-but-known peers pass, so a legacy-hidden contact's unread count survives indefinitely.
findings.approach: Add a one-time startup migration that clears unread counters (NOT history) for all currently-archived contacts, mirroring purgeStrangerDmCounters but keyed on archivedAt. Idempotent.

findings.text: purgeStrangerContacts's { deleted: number } return-value contract is unspecced on the SSR/storage-unavailable early-return path
findings.motivation: The function's doc comment promises @returns { deleted: number } always, and a caller could reasonably display this count in UI, but no AC in epic-dm-walled-garden (AC-PURGE-5, which specs the sweep's deletion behavior) or elsewhere pins the exact shape of the early-return value when storage is unavailable. A mutation-testing gate (2026-07-14, invite-group-member-from-contacts pre-ship audit) found the { deleted: 0 } object literal could be mutated to {} with zero test failures until a new assertion was added ad hoc. A human should decide whether to formalize this contract in an AC (e.g. add to epic-dm-walled-garden's Retroactive Purge section) or leave it as implementation-only.
findings.behavior: purgeStrangerContacts(getWhitelist) returns exactly { deleted: 0 } (not undefined-deleted or {}) when isStorageAvailable() is false. A closing test now asserts this in app/tests/unit/contacts-ssr-guard.test.ts.

findings.text: Invite-modal picker cannot re-invite a contact who is already invited but hasn't accepted — they show as disabled 'already in group' with no path to resend
findings.motivation: Originally locked product decision (epic invite-group-member-from-contacts, DD-4) wanted a pending-but-not-yet-joined contact to stay selectable so an admin could resend a lost/expired invite. Live e2e testing proved this can't work as specced: group.memberPubkeys updates the moment inviteByNpub succeeds (an MLS commit), not when the invitee accepts, so the picker's already-member check can't currently tell 'invited, still pending' apart from 'fully joined'.
findings.behavior: Today: after A invites B, B's picker option becomes disabled ('already in group') and stays that way until B accepts or the invite is otherwise resolved (e.g. cancelled). There is no way to resend via the picker while pending.
findings.approach: The app already computes the right signal for this — groups.tsx's local confirmedPubkeys (a per-member 'has this person sent a profile message in this group yet' Set, currently only used to drive the member-list's Pending badge). Thread that signal (or an equivalent) through to a shared, contacts.ts-reachable form so the invite-picker predicate can distinguish 'pending invite' from 'confirmed member' and re-enable a distinct pending state per the original DD-4 intent.
findings.success_signal: Re-opening the invite picker for a group where a contact was invited but hasn't accepted shows that contact as selectable (not disabled), and resubmitting the invite succeeds.

findings.text: dm-self-heal.spec.ts AC-29: malformed JSON-envelope content row not self-healed to decoded text
findings.motivation: Full e2e gate (make test-e2e-all) failed on this AC during an unrelated epic's pre-ship gate — first time this specific AC-29 failure has been observed, so it needs triage rather than being assumed to be the known chronic shared-relay flake.
findings.behavior: AC-29 expects a malformed JSON-envelope message row to self-heal to decoded text ('hello from envelope') and survive reload with the IDB row rewritten in place. Instead the raw JSON envelope string renders in the bubble and the assertion times out after 30s.
findings.success_signal: dm-self-heal.spec.ts AC-29 passes in an isolated run (not just within the full sequential gate, to rule out cross-test relay/keypair contamination).

findings.text: initJoinRequestCounts' startup-scan reconciliation has no AC in epic-group-invite-links
findings.motivation: epic-group-invite-links AC-6 ('Unread Store Join Request Counters') specifies the join-request counter API surface — incrementJoinRequest, markJoinRequestsRead, clearJoinRequestGroup, totalUnread, useUnreadCounts — but says nothing about initJoinRequestCounts, the startup scan that rebuilds the badge from persisted pending requests on app load. The unspecified user story a mutation-gate survivor (Stryker id 117, unreadStore.ts:317) exposed: 'a join request that arrives while the app is still loading its badge counts must still show up in the badge.' The chat-message slice's equivalent invariant is asserted (unreadStore.test.ts:283) and the DM slice's is too; the join-request slice was the only one of the three with no test, because no AC ever named the behavior. A property has now been written against it (unreadStore.test.ts, describe 'a join request arriving during the startup scan still reaches the badge'), but the contract it asserts is inferred from sibling slices rather than specified.
findings.behavior: initJoinRequestCounts scans persisted pending join requests per group and replaces the joinRequests slice. A request that lands mid-scan (welcomeSubscription persists via savePendingJoinRequest, then calls incrementJoinRequest) is protected from the stale scan snapshot by noteLiveIncrement('joinRequests', groupId), which queues the group for an authoritative re-read. Before this pass, blanking that slice-name literal to '' disabled the protection with no test failing — the mid-scan request was silently dropped from the badge until the next app load.
findings.approach: Spec-then-property: the property exists and passes; AC-6 should gain a bullet naming initJoinRequestCounts and the mid-scan-arrival invariant so the contract is specified rather than inferred. Consider whether the same startup-reconciliation invariant deserves one shared AC covering all three unreadStore slices (counts / joinRequests / directMessages) rather than a per-epic restatement.

findings.text: invite-links.md still specifies mute/unmute for invite links, but shipped behaviour is automatic ~24h expiry plus terminal delete (epic-invite-link-lifecycle) — the spec is stale
findings.motivation: The spec is a misleading source of truth: it documents a reversible mute mechanism that no longer exists, so anyone (or any agent) researching invite-link behaviour from it reasons about and documents the wrong behaviour. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/group-administration.md.
findings.behavior: invite-links.md (Nonce-based revocation / Flow 4) describes muting and unmuting a nonce. Shipped code implements automatic expiry (inviteLinkStorage.ts expiresAt = createdAt + DAY_MS, isExpired) and terminal hard-delete only (ManageInviteLinksModal deleteInviteLink; joinRequestHandler drops expired and muted nonces), per epic-invite-link-lifecycle (epic-state: done). invite-links.md is not marked superseded.
findings.approach: Update invite-links.md to defer to epic-invite-link-lifecycle for the current lifecycle, or mark the muting section superseded.

findings.text: Two co-admins leaving at nearly the same moment can strand a group with zero admins; no spec/AC/ADR records or accepts this
findings.motivation: A group left with zero admins is permanently frozen for membership changes (no invite/remove/rename/grant). The last-admin leave guard is presented as preventing exactly this, so the gap between its intent and its actual guarantee is a silent, unrecorded correctness risk. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/group-administration.md.
findings.behavior: The sole-admin leave block (selectLeaveModalState -> isSoleAdmin) is evaluated against each leaver's most-recently-known adminPubkeys, and leaveGroupImpl performs no admin re-check at execution time. Two admins leaving within the same brief window each pass the block independently and the group can reach zero admins. No spec, acceptance criterion, or ADR records this race or accepts it as a known risk.
findings.approach: Decide and record the intended behaviour: accept as a documented risk, or add an execution-time re-check/guard. Capture in the admin-role epic or an ADR.

findings.text: If a leaver's kind-13 departure signal fails to send, they purge locally but persist as a normal roster entry forever, with no pending-cleanup state and nothing for an admin to finalise
findings.motivation: This is a silent, unrecoverable divergence between a member's real state (gone) and every remaining member's roster (present, indistinguishable from an active member). Unlike the normal ghost window it never resolves and shows no pending indicator, so remaining members are misled indefinitely. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/group-administration.md.
findings.behavior: leaveGroupImpl catches a failed kind-13 send and proceeds with the local purge (soft-leave). When the send fails (e.g. the leaver is offline), no departure signal ever reaches the group: no admin can finalise the removal and no leave-pending/removal-pending state is shown. The out-of-band-leave spec covers the ghost window assuming the signal was sent, but not the send-failed case.
findings.approach: Record the behaviour and decide whether to mitigate (retry/queue the kind-13 until sent, or surface a local indicator) or accept it as a documented risk in the out-of-band-leave spec.

findings.text: cancelPendingInvitation can evict a just-joined member: 'pending' is derived (in MLS tree + no profile) and cannot tell a not-yet-accepted direct invite from an approved join-requester whose profile has not yet propagated
findings.motivation: Admin-facing footgun that removes a real, consenting member. isPendingMemberImpl treats anyone in the MLS tree without a received profile as pending, and the cancel action is offered for all of them. A join-requester who supplied no name has no provisional profile, so during the window between joining and their profile arriving they look cancellable and cancelling evicts them. The scope-withdraw-invite pre-spec was written to fix exactly this but is unshipped. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/groups.md.
findings.behavior: cancelPendingInvitationImpl commits a real MLS Remove for any pubkey that isPendingMemberImpl reports as pending (in tree, no non-provisional profile). No marker or origin field distinguishes a direct-invite join from an approved-join-request join, so the derived-pending state conflates them. Named approved requesters get a provisional profile (so they read as confirmed and are safe); nameless ones do not, and are removable during the profile-lag window.
findings.approach: Implement the scope-withdraw-invite pre-spec (a positive direct-invite marker so cancel is offered only for unaccepted direct invites), or otherwise record join-origin; alternatively seed a provisional profile for nameless approved requesters so they read as confirmed immediately.

findings.text: Group profile-sync (marmot/profileSync.ts payloadToMemberProfile) accepts any avatar.imageUrl from the wire with no scheme allow-list or length cap, unlike the 1:1 dmProfile channel which validates it
findings.motivation: A group member can put a javascript: or data: URL in their profile's avatar and it is stored and later used as an image source for other members — a latent injection/XSS-adjacent vector. The 1:1 profile codec deliberately validates avatar URL scheme (https:// or //) and the code comment in dmProfile/kinds.ts explicitly notes the group path does NOT, so the asymmetry is known but untracked. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/profiles-and-identity.md.
findings.behavior: payloadToMemberProfile reads payload.avatar.imageUrl and stores it as-is with no validation; dmProfile/kinds.ts validates scheme (https/protocol-relative) and rejects malformed avatars, and its own doc comment flags that the MLS group profile-sync mechanism performs no such check. Group member profiles are trusted-member-attested only (ADR-006), so a member can inject an arbitrary avatar URL.
findings.approach: Apply the same avatar-URL scheme allow-list and length cap used by the 1:1 dmProfile codec to payloadToMemberProfile before persisting a member profile.

findings.text: You cannot re-add a reaction emoji you previously removed from a message: the reaction store's removal-wins rule permanently discards any later add for the same (message, reactor, emoji), so the re-add shows only locally and reaches no peer
findings.motivation: A silent, confusing UX defect: a user removes 👍 from a message, later taps 👍 again, sees it appear on their own device, but no other participant ever receives it and the local row stays permanently unconfirmed. The user has no indication their re-reaction went nowhere. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/reactions-and-emoji.md.
findings.behavior: The receive/merge path enforces 'removal wins regardless of arrival order' (api.ts ~401-417) to survive out-of-order relay delivery: once a (messageId, reactor, emoji) triple is tombstoned, any later add rumor for the same triple is discarded with no write. The optimistic local insert uses a fresh rumor id and shows on the sender's device, but every peer — and the sender's own inbound echo — discards it, leaving the row optimistic-only (eventId empty) forever. Re-adding a DIFFERENT emoji works; re-adding the same emoji is dead.
findings.approach: Decide the intended semantics: either allow re-adding a removed emoji (e.g. make add-wins when the add is strictly newer than the tombstone, keeping out-of-order protection via timestamps/rev), or surface that a removed emoji cannot be re-added. Today's removal-wins rule is order-independent but blocks legitimate re-reactions.

findings.text: Poll vote for a not-yet-received poll is silently dropped, contradicting group-polls AC-9 which specified queueing early votes until the poll-open arrives
findings.motivation: Under out-of-order MLS delivery a member's vote can arrive before the poll-open they were reacting to; the spec (group-polls.md edge cases / AC-9) requires such votes to be queued and applied when the poll arrives, so no vote is lost to arrival order. The shipped handler drops them, so a legitimately-cast vote can silently fail to count. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/polls.md.
findings.behavior: pollHandler drops a vote whose poll is unknown (if (!existingPoll) return), with no queue anywhere; the unit test pollHandler.test.ts asserts saveVote is NOT called for an unknown poll. The close handler similarly drops a close for an unknown poll. AC-9 / group-polls.md specify queue-then-apply.
findings.approach: Implement the specified pending-vote queue keyed by pollId (bounded), applied when the matching poll-open arrives; or amend the spec/AC to accept drop-on-unknown-poll as the intended behaviour.

findings.text: Poll receive-side handlers do not validate under the member-attested trust model: a member can forge a close as the creator (replacing everyone's authoritative tally) or cast a single-choice vote listing multiple options (counted multiple times); the parser imposes no option-count/length caps
findings.motivation: The close message carries an authoritative tally that replaces each member's locally-computed result, so a forged close (author checked only against the self-reported pubkey, per ADR-006) lets any member inject an arbitrary result for everyone — higher impact than a forged vote. Separately, the vote parser never checks a single-choice poll's responses count, so a crafted multi-response vote inflates several options at once. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/polls.md.
findings.behavior: Close authorization compares poll.creatorPubkey to the rumor's self-reported pubkey only (no cryptographic sender binding — marmot-ts drops the MLS sender leaf, ADR-006). parsePollVote requires >=1 response but does not enforce single-choice (<=1) against poll type, and tallying counts every response. parsePollOpen checks only >=2 options — no upper bound or length caps on receipt (caps are creation-side only).
findings.approach: On receipt: reject a close whose tally is recomputed-mismatched or (longer term) bind authorship when marmot-ts exposes the sender leaf; enforce single-choice response-count against poll type before tallying; apply the option-count and length caps on the receive side too. Accept residual member-on-member risk per ADR-006 but remove the amplifiers (tally injection, multi-count).

findings.text: relay-backup.md Section 9 specifies a destructive clear-and-replace restore, but shipped restore is a non-destructive merge (relayBackup.ts restoreFromBackup documents the earlier blanket-clear as a fixed bug) — the spec is stale
findings.motivation: The spec is a misleading source of truth: it tells a reader/agent that restore wipes and replaces all local state, when the shipped code deliberately preserves everything the backup does not carry (contacts, DMs, knownPeers, relays, signerMode) and merges group chat by message id. Anyone reasoning from the spec would document or reason about data-loss behaviour that no longer exists. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/backup-and-recovery.md.
findings.behavior: relay-backup.md Section 9 says restore 'always overwrites local state completely / clear existing local state'. restoreFromBackup in app/src/lib/backup/relayBackup.ts overwrites only backup-carried categories (settings, profile), adds/updates groups without deleting others, and merges group chat (local wins on id collision), with a header comment noting the earlier clear-everything approach was a bug. The spec is not marked superseded.
findings.approach: Update relay-backup.md Section 9 to describe the non-destructive add/update/merge restore, or mark it superseded and point to the implementation as authoritative.

findings.text: State-restore reports success even when nothing was restored: an unreachable relay, a missing backup, and a too-new (rejected) backup are all silently swallowed and the flow completes as identity-only recovery with no error, indistinguishable to the user
findings.motivation: A user who recovers their identity and expects their groups/settings to return cannot tell whether the state-restore actually happened. A relay outage or a version-incompatible backup produces the same silent 'success' as 'no backup existed', so a failed restore looks like a completed one — the user may proceed believing their state will come back when it will not. Surfaced while writing docs/behavior/backup-and-recovery.md.
findings.behavior: settings.tsx handleRestore wraps the backup fetch in try/catch; on any failure (relay unreachable, fetch error, or fetchBackup throwing on version !== 1) it console.warns and falls through to setRestoreSuccess(true). Identity restore completes and the user sees a success outcome with no state restored and no distinct error for the three cases.
findings.approach: Distinguish and surface the outcomes: 'backup restored', 'no backup found', 'relays unreachable — retry', and 'backup from a newer version — not applied' should be separate user-visible results rather than one silent success.

findings.text: Profile-page 'Add to Group' success confirmation is structurally unreachable in the common single-addable-group case
findings.motivation: A user who adds a contact to a group from the contact's profile page gets no success feedback — the confirmation Alert flashes and vanishes, so the click appears to do nothing even though the invite succeeded. Surfaced by the invite-rescind-and-member-removal epic's SCEN-3 e2e (the profile-page direct-invite entry point).
findings.behavior: handleAddToGroup awaits inviteByNpub, which internally awaits reloadGroups() before resolving. The added contact thus becomes a (pending) member and addableGroupsForContact returns empty BEFORE inviteByNpub resolves. The entire Add-to-Group widget — select, button, AND the success/error Alerts — is gated on addableGroups.length > 0 (profile.tsx:673), so the whole block (success Alert included) unmounts in the same render pass that would have shown the success state. When the contact has exactly one addable group (the common/tested case), the success confirmation is structurally unreachable through the real UI.
findings.approach: Render the success/error confirmation OUTSIDE the addableGroups.length > 0 gate, or defer/decouple the membership refresh from the confirmation render so the Alert can show before addableGroups empties. Pre-existing (the widget gating predates the epic); the invite + marker-write themselves work correctly.
findings.success_signal: Adding a contact to a group from their profile page shows a visible success confirmation even when it was their only addable group

archive.text: onMembersChanged fires only when currentMembers.length differs from stored.memberPubkeys.length; a future ghost-cleanup pass that removes a ghost but adds a real member in the same commit would leave the departed member visible in the UI even after AC-MEMBERS-1 passes.

Profile-request memo.attempts stays at 0 under Playwright fake-clock cycling — AC-045 scenario 5 deferred (test.fixme). Real cause undiagnosed; candidates: fake-clock + setInterval interaction, recordEmitted Date.now() racing real-time write, or requestedOnEntryForRef cycling under cross-navigation re-mount. Boundary contract already vitest-covered.
archive.reason: Retry-cap behavior (1h/7d/UNANSWERED_MAX_ATTEMPTS=3) is deterministically covered by profileRequestStorage.integration.test.ts (8 tests). The e2e scenario-5 variant is redundant and blocked by an undiagnosed Playwright page.clock+timer interaction (two prior diagnoses already proved wrong); chasing it adds no behavioral coverage. Leave test.fixme in place.

archive.text: groups+dm e2e suite scale-bleed: 177-test single-run cumulative had a 1-test failure at #130 (groups-profile-request.spec.ts:149 scenario 1 'aged-history backfill AC-045'), but the same scenario passes in 2.6s when run in isolation. After ~10min the dev server's HMR cache, strfry's tmpfs event log, and per-context IDB state accumulate enough drift to make subscription-driven assertions miss their deadline. Generalises the scenario-5 finding (profile-request-memo-attempts-stays-at) — same root pattern, different AC-045 scenario surfacing it.
archive.reason: Did not reproduce: the named test (groups-profile-request scenario 1, aged-history AC-045) passed in 9.3s at position #168 of a clean 216-test/26-min full groups run this session (0 failures). Generalises the already-rejected scenario-5 finding; the AC-045 behaviors are deterministically unit-covered (profileRequestStorage.integration.test.ts). Intermittent e2e-infra drift, not a product bug; test-isolation hardening is uncertain-payoff for a non-reproducing flake.

archive.text: ContactChat.tsx uses the same fetch-then-live-sub anti-pattern fixed in welcomeSubscription.ts: Phase-1 runs three parallel fetchEventsWithTimeout calls for DM history then Phase-2 opens three live ndk.subscribe calls, leaving a gap where events are silently lost.
archive.reason: The welcomeSubscription since-anchor fix does NOT transfer: ContactChat's primary DM path is kind-1059 gift-wraps whose outer created_at is randomized/backdated up to 2 days (NIP-59 privacy), so a since:fetchStartedAt filter on the gift-wrap live sub would DROP incoming DMs — a regression. since is only safe on the legacy kind-4 subs (real timestamps), whose inbound-only gap is negligible. Existing per-id dedup (ingestEvent/knownMessageIds + shouldIngestRumor) already absorbs the small overlap. Closing the gap properly would need a relay-replay mechanism that doesn't key on the spoofed gift-wrap timestamp — out of scope for a smell-driven refactor.
